content:
# Matthew Chapter 19 -- Disagreement Log
**Arbiter:** SOPHIA | **In council with:** EMET, LOGOS, PNEUMA
**Date:** 2026-03-05

This log records all verses in Matthew Chapter 19 where the three translators (EMET, LOGOS, PNEUMA) significantly diverged in their renderings, along with SOPHIA's resolution and reasoning.

---

## 19:3 -- "for any cause" vs. "for every cause" vs. "for any reason"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "for every cause" | Literal kata pasan aitian |
| LOGOS | "for any cause" | Formal equivalent |
| PNEUMA | "for any reason" | Dynamic equivalent |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted LOGOS's "for any cause." The phrase kata pasan aitian (literally "according to every cause") is a technical reference to the debate between the schools of Hillel and Shammai over divorce grounds. "Any cause" and "every cause" are functionally identical; "any reason" (PNEUMA) is acceptable but "cause" better preserves the legal/halakhic register.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS.

---

## 19:5 -- "hold fast to" vs. "be joined to" vs. "be united to"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "will be joined to the wife of him" | Literal kollethsetai (to be glued/joined) |
| LOGOS | "hold fast to his wife" | Formal equivalent emphasizing tenacity |
| PNEUMA | "be united to his wife" | Dynamic equivalent |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted LOGOS's "hold fast to." The Greek proskollao means to be glued to, to cling to. "Hold fast" captures the tenacious, permanent quality of the union. "Be joined" (EMET) and "be united" (PNEUMA) are adequate but less forceful. The Genesis 2:24 quotation is foundational, and the verb's strength should be preserved.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS.

---

## 19:6 -- "joined together" vs. "yoked-together"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "What therefore the God yoked-together" | Literal sunezeuzen (yoked together) |
| LOGOS | "What therefore God has joined together" | Standard rendering |
| PNEUMA | "what God has joined together" | Same as LOGOS |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted LOGOS/PNEUMA's "joined together." The Greek suzeugnumi literally means "to yoke together" (from zugos, yoke), and EMET's rendering preserves this agricultural metaphor. However, "joined together" is the universally recognized English rendering of this marriage formula. The yoke imagery is noted.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS/PNEUMA. The yoke etymology is preserved in notes.

---

## 19:8 -- "allowed" vs. "permitted" vs. "permitted"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "Moses...permitted you to divorce the wives of you" | Literal epetrepsen |
| LOGOS | "Moses allowed you to divorce your wives" | Formal with "allowed" |
| PNEUMA | "Moses permitted divorce only because of your hard hearts" | Dynamic restructuring |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted LOGOS's "allowed." Both "allowed" and "permitted" are valid for epetrepsen. The key distinction is between "commanded" (v.7, the Pharisees' claim) and "allowed" (v.8, Jesus' correction). PNEUMA adds "only" which is not in the Greek but is interpretively correct -- the concession is narrow.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS.

---

## 19:9 -- "except for sexual immorality" vs. "except for sexual-immorality" vs. "unless the marriage is unlawful"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "except for sexual-immorality" | Literal porneia rendered broadly |
| LOGOS | "except for sexual immorality" | Same as EMET |
| PNEUMA | "unless the marriage is unlawful" | Interprets porneia as unlawful marriage (incest) |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted EMET/LOGOS's "except for sexual immorality." The Greek porneia is a broad term covering various forms of sexual sin. PNEUMA's "unless the marriage is unlawful" adopts one specific scholarly interpretation (that porneia here means marriages within prohibited degrees of kinship, per Leviticus 18). While this is a defensible position, it narrows the meaning to one interpretive option. The consensus text should preserve the range of porneia.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of EMET/LOGOS. PNEUMA's interpretation is noted but not adopted as the primary rendering.

---

## 19:10 -- "it is better not to marry" vs. "it is not profitable to marry"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "it is not profitable to marry" | Literal ou sumpherei (it is not advantageous) |
| LOGOS | "it is better not to marry" | Standard English rendering |
| PNEUMA | "it's better not to marry" | Same as LOGOS |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted LOGOS/PNEUMA's "better not to marry." The Greek sumphero means "it is advantageous/profitable/expedient." "Better" is a natural English equivalent. EMET's "profitable" has commercial overtones that sound jarring in a marriage discussion.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS/PNEUMA.

---

## 19:12 -- "eunuchs who have made themselves eunuchs" vs. "some choose not to marry"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "there are eunuchs who made themselves eunuchs on account of the kingdom of the heavens" | Literal with full eunuch language |
| LOGOS | "there are eunuchs who have made themselves eunuchs for the sake of the kingdom of heaven" | Formal -- retains eunuch language |
| PNEUMA | "And some choose not to marry for the sake of the kingdom of heaven" | Dynamic -- interprets eunuch as celibacy choice |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted LOGOS's rendering. Jesus deliberately uses the stark term "eunuch" (eunouchos) three times in this verse, and each usage refers to a different category. PNEUMA's interpretation of the third category as "choose not to marry" is correct in meaning but replaces Jesus' provocative, countercultural language with a sanitized paraphrase. The shock value of a rabbi commending voluntary celibacy using the term "eunuch" is part of the text's force.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS. Jesus' deliberate use of "eunuch" language is preserved.

---

## 19:17 -- "Why do you ask me about what is good?" (Sinaiticus reading)

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "Why do you ask me concerning the good? One is the good." | Literal Sinaiticus reading |
| LOGOS | "Why do you ask me about what is good? There is only one who is good." | Formal equivalent of Sinaiticus |
| PNEUMA | "Why ask me what is good? Only God is good." | Dynamic -- explicitly identifies "the good one" as God |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted LOGOS's rendering. All three correctly follow the Sinaiticus reading (different from Mark's "Why do you call me good?"). PNEUMA adds "God" explicitly, which is an interpretive identification not present in the text. The Greek says "one is the good" (heis estin ho agathos) without naming God directly.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS. PNEUMA's explicit "God" goes beyond the text.

---

## 19:21 -- "perfect" vs. "complete"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "If you wish to be perfect" | Literal teleios |
| LOGOS | "If you would be perfect" | Same as EMET |
| PNEUMA | "If you want to be complete" | Dynamic -- "complete" for teleios |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted EMET/LOGOS's "perfect." The Greek teleios means "complete, mature, perfect." While PNEUMA's "complete" captures one dimension of the word (wholeness), "perfect" is the established English rendering that connects to the Sermon on the Mount's teleios in 5:48 ("Be perfect, as your heavenly Father is perfect"). Consistency across Matthew is important.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of EMET/LOGOS for consistency with 5:48.

---

## 19:28 -- "in the new world" vs. "in the regeneration" vs. "when the world is made new"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "in the regeneration" | Literal palingenesia |
| LOGOS | "in the new world" | Interpretive formal rendering |
| PNEUMA | "when the world is made new" | Dynamic paraphrase |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted LOGOS's "in the new world." The Greek palingenesia (lit. "rebirth/regeneration") is used only here and in Titus 3:5 in the NT. EMET's "regeneration" is literal but could be confused with individual spiritual rebirth (as in Titus). LOGOS's "new world" captures the cosmic scope -- the renewal of all creation. PNEUMA's "when the world is made new" is a temporal paraphrase that conveys the meaning well but is wordy.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS. The cosmic scope of palingenesia is preserved.

---

## 19:22 -- "sorrowful" vs. "grieving" vs. "sad"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "the young man went away grieving" | Literal lupoumenos |
| LOGOS | "he went away sorrowful" | Formal equivalent |
| PNEUMA | "he walked away sad" | Dynamic simplification |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted LOGOS's "sorrowful." The Greek lupoumenos (present passive participle of lupeo) conveys deep grief, not mere sadness. "Sorrowful" captures the weight of the moment -- the young man is not mildly disappointed but genuinely grieved. PNEUMA's "sad" is too light for the narrative gravity.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS.

---

## 19:24 -- "go through the eye of a needle" vs. "squeeze through the eye of a needle"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "a camel to go through [the] eye of a needle" | Literal dielthein |
| LOGOS | "a camel to go through the eye of a needle" | Same as EMET |
| PNEUMA | "a camel to squeeze through the eye of a needle" | Dynamic -- "squeeze" for "go through" |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted EMET/LOGOS's "go through." The Greek dielthein means "to pass through." PNEUMA's "squeeze" adds a sense of struggle and tight passage that softens the image -- Jesus' point is impossibility, not extreme difficulty. A camel cannot "squeeze through" a needle's eye; the image is meant to be absurd.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of EMET/LOGOS.

---

## 19:30 -- "many who are first will be last" vs. "many who seem to be first will end up last"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "many first will be last, and last first" | Literal compressed form |
| LOGOS | "many who are first will be last, and the last first" | Formal equivalent |
| PNEUMA | "many who seem to be first will end up last, and many who seem to be last will end up first" | Dynamic with "seem" added |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted LOGOS's rendering. PNEUMA's addition of "seem to be" interprets the saying: it suggests that current status is merely apparent. While that may be implied, the Greek makes a starker statement -- those who are first (not "seem to be") will be last. The reversal is real, not merely perspectival.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS. PNEUMA's "seem to be" softens the reversal.

---

## Summary of Disagreement Patterns

| Pattern | Count | Typical Resolution |
|---|---|---|
| PNEUMA dynamic paraphrase vs. LOGOS/EMET | 9 | LOGOS preferred |
| PNEUMA narrows interpretive range | 2 | Broader rendering retained (porneia, v.17 "God") |
| PNEUMA adds words not in Greek | 3 | Additions rejected ("only," "seem," "God") |
| Consistency with earlier chapters | 2 | Maintained (teleios/5:48, ekklesia/16:18) |
| EMET literalism too stark for English | 2 | LOGOS preferred (profitable, yoked) |
| All three agree | 2 | v.17 Sinaiticus reading, v.24 camel/needle image |

### Overall Pattern Analysis

Chapter 19 addresses sensitive topics (divorce, celibacy, wealth) where interpretive choices carry significant weight. The most consequential disagreement is over porneia in v.9, where PNEUMA's "unless the marriage is unlawful" adopts a specific scholarly interpretation that narrows the Greek's semantic range. SOPHIA preserves the broader "sexual immorality" to avoid predetermining the reader's interpretation of this long-debated exception clause. The teleios/perfect decision (v.21) demonstrates the importance of cross-chapter consistency: the same word must be rendered the same way in 5:48 and 19:21 to preserve Matthew's theological vocabulary. The palingenesia decision (v.28) is one of the chapter's most interesting -- a genuinely rare NT word where LOGOS's "new world" best captures the cosmic eschatological scope that EMET's "regeneration" risks narrowing to individual experience. PNEUMA's pattern of adding qualifying words ("seem," "only," "squeeze") continues to be the most frequent reason for divergence from the consensus.

---

*Log compiled by SOPHIA, AI Translator and Arbiter, 2026-03-05*
